19. Information Geometry of ML and EM

Recall the EM algorithm (Section 12) is an iterative procedure for obtaining ML and MAP (ps4.4) estimates. We show that it is equivalent to an alternating divergence minimization procedure.

19.1. ML as a Reverse I-Projection

Focus on iid discrete data and a nonrandom paratmer . With such i.i.d. models, they are permutation invariant and the ordering of the values doesn’t matter. Hence a sufficient statistic23 is the empirical distribution or type of the data (fraction of times each symbol appears in the sequence):

i.e. the distribution minimizing

This is called a reverse I-projection since is in the other slot, or sometimes, M-projection.

□

Hence maximizing the likelihood, minimizing the divergence, and minimizing the are equivalent descriptions of the same optimization.

Proposition 19.1. The conditional empirical distribution associated with matches with the actual conditional distribution .
Proof

From the saturation condition of DPI, we have the following result:

Actually we work the other way around: we define to satisfy the equality condition of the DPI and verify that it still leads to a normalized distribution so that it is the KL divergence minimizer.

[TODO] Is this a coincidence that the equality condition for the DPI coincides with the set of allowed z-distributions? Nah it’s like the DPI picks the one that further satisfies the condition of matching the conditionals. If the conditionals match then the marginals match, which is the original condition.

□

Then the expectation in is with respect to a computed distribution, and maximizing it is simply minimizing a KL divergence.
